title: Where All Your Money Actually Goes

beats.narration: You'll earn about two million dollars in your life. So why does it never feel like enough? Watch where almost all of it quietly disappears.
beats.understand: cold open: a paycheck lands and the money instantly starts leaking away

facts.claim: A typical worker earns about $2 million over a lifetime
facts.source: U.S. Census Bureau / Social Security Administration lifetime-earnings estimates
facts.assumptions: Rounded illustrative figure; full-time worker over a ~40-year career in nominal dollars. Median lifetime earnings are ~$1.7M and higher with a bachelor's degree.

beats.narration: In the end, only about fifteen cents of every dollar you earn is ever really yours.
beats.understand: a stark stat card teasing the punchline of the whole video

beats.narration: Two million dollars. On paper, it's a fortune. A mansion, a boat, a life of never checking a price tag. But in reality you never really hold it, because it's spoken for long before it ever lands in your account.
beats.understand: a real photo of cash — the raw number, before reality takes its cut

image.query: stacks of cash money hundred dollar bills

beats.narration: Chapter one. The government's cut.
beats.understand: chapter title card

beats.narration: Before you hold a single dollar, a share is already gone. Income tax, payroll tax, and sales tax on almost everything you buy with what's left.
beats.understand: the figure hands a stack of cash through a cold government window

beats.narration: Across a lifetime, that adds up to roughly five hundred thousand dollars, handed over a little at a time. A full quarter of everything you will ever make, gone before you can even decide what to do with it.
beats.understand: a real photo grounding the tax point — a government building

facts.claim: Lifetime taxes total roughly $500,000 — about a quarter of earnings
facts.source: Bureau of Labor Statistics & Tax Foundation effective-tax-burden estimates
facts.assumptions: Combined federal income, payroll, state, and sales tax; ~25% average effective burden applied to the $2M lifetime baseline.

beats.narration: Two million, minus taxes. One and a half million left.
beats.understand: the recurring money bar: taxes drain out

beats.narration: Chapter two. A roof over your head.
beats.understand: chapter title card

beats.narration: Now the biggest bite of all. Rent or a mortgage takes about a third of every paycheck, month after month, for decades.
beats.understand: the figure stands small in a lit doorway; rent floats out the chimney each month

beats.narration: You don't really buy a home. For most of your life you rent it, from a landlord or from a bank. And when you add up every payment, every repair, and every year of property tax, just having somewhere to sleep swallows another half a million dollars.
beats.understand: a real photo of houses grounding the housing point

facts.claim: Housing takes about a third of spending — roughly $500,000 over a lifetime
facts.source: BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey (housing ~33% of average annual expenditures)
facts.assumptions: National-average spending share applied to lifetime income net of tax; rent or mortgage plus repairs, insurance, and property tax.

beats.narration: And just like that, we're down to one million dollars.
beats.understand: the money bar: housing drains out

beats.narration: Chapter three. Getting around.
beats.understand: chapter title card

beats.narration: Then there's simply getting from place to place. Car payments, insurance, gas, and repairs — the machine that carries you to work quietly eats the money you go to work for.
beats.understand: the figure at a gas pump, the dollar meter racing up

image.query: highway traffic cars rush hour aerial

beats.narration: A car spends most of its life parked, losing value, while you keep paying for it. Over a lifetime of commutes, getting around costs about a quarter of a million dollars.
beats.understand: a real photo of traffic grounding the transport point

facts.claim: Getting around costs about a quarter of a million dollars over a lifetime
facts.source: BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey (transportation ~16% of spending)
facts.assumptions: National-average share; car payments, insurance, fuel, and repairs; illustrative rounded total.

beats.narration: Seven hundred and fifty thousand left.
beats.understand: the money bar: getting around drains out

beats.narration: Chapter four. Staying alive.
beats.understand: chapter title card

beats.narration: Now the one you can't skip. Groceries, restaurants, the coffee on the way in. It never feels like much at the register, but it quietly becomes a fortune.
beats.understand: the figure at a checkout, the total climbing, the receipt printing longer

image.query: grocery cart in a supermarket aisle

beats.narration: Three meals a day, every day, for a whole lifetime. Feeding yourself alone runs around three hundred thousand dollars over the years.
beats.understand: a real photo of groceries grounding the food point

image.query: cup of coffee on a cafe table morning

facts.claim: Feeding yourself runs about $300,000 over a lifetime
facts.source: BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey (food at home + away ~12–13% of spending)
facts.assumptions: National-average share for a single person; groceries and restaurants; illustrative rounded total.

beats.narration: Four hundred and fifty thousand now.
beats.understand: the money bar: food drains out

image.query: friends eating at a restaurant table

beats.narration: Chapter five. The little leaks.
beats.understand: chapter title card

beats.narration: And then the leaks you barely notice. Streaming, subscriptions, fees, the free trial you forgot to cancel — draining every month whether you use them or not.
beats.understand: the figure on a couch ringed by subscription tiles, each siphoning a coin

beats.narration: Alone, each one is nothing. Together, they siphon off a hundred and fifty thousand dollars, leaving just three hundred thousand.
beats.understand: the money bar: the little leaks drain out

facts.claim: Subscriptions and recurring fees siphon off about $150,000 over a lifetime
facts.source: Consumer subscription-spending surveys (C+R Research; West Monroe)
facts.assumptions: Illustrative aggregate of streaming, memberships, and auto-renewing fees compounded over a working life.

image.query: credit card and smartphone online payment

beats.narration: Chapter six. The trap.
beats.understand: chapter title card

beats.narration: Here's the cruel part. When you finally earn more, you don't keep more. You just buy a nicer car and a bigger place, and the money drains right back out.
beats.understand: the figure runs on a treadmill going nowhere as money pours in and drains out

beats.narration: It's why so many people who earn a lot still feel like they have nothing. The trap isn't how much you make — it's that the spending quietly grows to match it, every single time you get ahead.
beats.understand: a real photo grounding the trap — a busy city of workers

image.query: crowd of people walking city street

beats.narration: Chapter seven. What's actually left.
beats.understand: chapter title card

beats.narration: So out of two million dollars, this is the part that's actually yours. Three hundred thousand.
beats.understand: the money bar resolves on the small glowing 'actually yours' sliver

facts.claim: About 15 cents of every dollar (~$300,000) remains as discretionary
facts.source: Derived residual: $2M earnings minus taxes, housing, transport, food, and recurring costs
facts.assumptions: Arithmetic residual of the figures above; an illustrative teaching device, not a measured personal savings rate.

beats.narration: Fifteen cents of every dollar you will ever earn, free to spend however you choose. It isn't much, but it's truly yours. So spend it on something that actually matters, because you paid for every last cent of it with your life.
beats.understand: close on real human moments — a life well spent

queries: hiker on a mountain summit at sunrise
